coredata.dc:title: Pulsed laser ablation based synthetic route for nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots using graphite flakes
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Sukhyun
Jeong, Young Kyu
Ryu, Jeong Ho
Son, Yong
Kim, Won Rae
Lee, Byoungsoo
Jung, Kyung Hwan
Kim, Kang Min
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) process face barriers to commercialization due to their poor optical properties and the use of expensive carbon precursors. In this work, we report a one-step route for the preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with excellent optical properties using a low-cost carbon precursor. The as-prepared NGQDs exhibited excellent optical properties and high quantum yield compared to pristine graphene quantum dots (0.8% → 9.1%) due to the presence of the N atoms. A possible recombination mechanism of NGQDs was investigated by time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy. The increase of N atoms incorporated in the GQDs resulted in an increased fraction of the short recombination lifetime from the intrinsic state. We also report a possible mechanism for the formation of the N atoms in the GQDs structure during the PLAL process, which is explained based on the plasma plume, cavitation collapse, and nitrogen precursor decomposition model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pulsed laser ablation
Graphene quantum dot
Nitrogen doped
Surface functionalization
Quantum yield

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of PGQDs, on-NGQDs and off-NGQDs. (a) UV–Vis spectra, (b) Photoluminescence (PL) properties under 360 nm excitation, and (c) PL excitation (PLE) spectra at 450 nm emission.
(a) XPS full survey spectra of PGQDs, on-NGQDs and off-NGQDs. (b) Representative schematic for the N configurations of the N-doped GQDs. XPS N1s spectra of the (c) on-NGQDs and (d) off-NGQDs.

(a) Representative schematic for the possible mechanism of the transformation of graphite flakes to off-NGQDs via the PLAL process. HR-TEM images of graphite flakes (b) before laser irradiation, (c) after laser irradiation for 5 min and (d) 30 min, respectively.

HR-TEM images of PGQDs and off-NGQDs. (a) TEM images of PGQDs and (b) off-NGQDs. HR-TEM images of (c) PGQDs and (d) off-NGQDs. Insets are the FFT pattern (right), which shows the high-quality crystalline hexagonal patterns of the GQDs. Left side insets are shows the lattice distance of the PGQDs and off-NGQDs.
TCSPC decay curves of the (a) PGQDs, (b) on-NGQDs, and (c) off-NGQDs. The solid lines are fitted by a tri-exponential function.
Excitation emission values, χ2 value, excitation lifetimes, and their corresponding amplitudes for PGQDs, on-NGQDs and off-NGQDs.

head.ce:title: Pulsed laser ablation based synthetic route for nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots using graphite flakes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, 137-41 Gwahakdanji-ro, Gangwon-do 25440, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, 137-41 Gwahakdanji-ro, Gangwon-do 25440, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, 50 Daehak-ro, Chungju-si, Chungbuk 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, 50 Daehak-ro, Chungju-si, Chungbuk 380-702, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, 113-58 Seohaean-ro, Siheung-si, Gyeonggi-do 15014, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, 113-58 Seohaean-ro, Siheung-si, Gyeonggi-do 15014, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These author’s contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) process face barriers to commercialization due to their poor optical properties and the use of expensive carbon precursors. In this work, we report a one-step route for the preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with excellent optical properties using a low-cost carbon precursor. The as-prepared NGQDs exhibited excellent optical properties and high quantum yield compared to pristine graphene quantum dots (0.8% → 9.1%) due to the presence of the N atoms. A possible recombination mechanism of NGQDs was investigated by time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy. The increase of N atoms incorporated in the GQDs resulted in an increased fraction of the short recombination lifetime from the intrinsic state. We also report a possible mechanism for the formation of the N atoms in the GQDs structure during the PLAL process, which is explained based on the plasma plume, cavitation collapse, and nitrogen precursor decomposition model.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum-sized particles have attracted considerable attention from researchers working on various applications such as those related to the optoelectronic and biomedical fields. In recent years, inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., Cd and Zn based quantum dots) have been intensively studied as promising optoelectronic materials owing to their extraordinary and unique properties such as bandgaps and narrow bandwidths that are easily controllable by controlling the size of the Quantum dots (QDs) during the synthesis process [1,2]. Nevertheless, the practical application of QDs remains severely limited due to their highly toxic and harmful effects on human cells [3–5].

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received much attention as the most suitable alternative due to their low toxicity, low cost, strong luminescence, and facile synthesis. Typically, GQDs have been fabricated mainly by a wet chemical route using expensive carbon precursors such as graphene, graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT), and carbon nanofiber (CNF) [6–8]. In addition, the wet chemical routes are usually performed under strong acidic conditions, which require complex, time-consuming washing steps [9–13]. Thus, it is necessary to develop a simple and cost-effective method for the fabrication of GQDs.

An alternative to the wet chemical method is pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) for the synthesis of GQDs. The PLAL method is facile, simple, and environmentally friendly because it does not require a harmful acidic chemical reactant and post-purification steps. Previously, our group proposed a PLAL process for preparing GQDs using coal as an abundantly available and inexpensive carbon precursor [14]. However, the as-prepared GQDs showed poorer optical properties (e.g., UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) intensity) compared to those produced by the chemical method. Hence, to overcome such issues, it is essential to develop a synthetic method that enhanced the optical properties of GQDs using the PLAL process.

From the perspective of the pulsed laser process, the nanoparticles prepared by pulsed laser ablation are highly dependent on the initial states of the precursor (e.g., solid target and/or dispersion in liquid medium) [15]. Thus, many researchers have attempted to modify the PLAL process to improve the optical properties of the prepared nanoparticles. Sasaki and co-workers proposed the synthesis of Au and ZnO nanoparticles via the PLAL process with ultrasonic waves [16]. The Au and ZnO nanoparticles exhibited enhanced absorbance due to the application of the ultrasonic waves during PLAL. Also, Hamad and co-workers reported the preparation of Ag–TiO2 nanoparticles via the PLAL process with a sonication bath. The prepared Ag–TiO2 nanoparticles exhibited significant enhancement of absorbance [17]. From the perspective of the structure and function, heteroatom (e.g., nitrogen) doped carbon structure were proposed to further improve the electronic characteristics [18]. Also, doping of GQDs with heteroatoms can effectively tune their intrinsic properties, including electronic characteristics and optical properties [19–23]. Consequently, the heteroatom-doped GQDs (e.g., nitrogen) exhibited enhanced optical properties (e.g., PL intensity, absorption) due to the presence of electrons with the withdrawing ability of N [23,24]. In both cases, it can be considered that this approach offers a way to improve the optical properties of GQDs.

Recently, Santiago and co-workers reported the synthesis of N-doped GQDs via PLAL process using graphene oxide (GO) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as carbon precursor and N dopant, respectively [23]. However, the high cost of carbon precursor (i.e., GO) has been the major barrier to the commercialization of GQDs. To overcome such issue, a relatively inexpensive graphite flakes can be one of the alternative carbon precursor to the synthesis of GQDs (Table S1). In addition, the formation mechanism of the N-doped GQDs from carbon precursor (i.e., GO) via PLAL process has not been presented in the above literature. Thus, it is also necessary to investigate the formation mechanism of N-doped GQDs via PLAL process.

Herein, we report a potentially cost-effective and easy approach to obtain highly luminescent N-doped GQDs via the PLAL process using inexpensive graphite flakes as the carbon precursor. We also discuss the role of high-power sonication in the synthesis of N-doped GQDs via the PLAL process. According to our investigation results, the N atoms can be effectively incorporated into the GQDs via the PLAL process, resulting in a greatly enhanced quantum yield (QY) of NGQDs after doping with N. The associated change in optical properties is investigated by time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy. Also, the chemical composition of the NGQDs is investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and the enhanced PL mechanism of the NGQDs is discussed.

Graphite flakes were purchased from HQ Graphene (Netherlands), and diethylenetriamine (DETA) and high-purity ethanol (>99.9%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. N-doped GQDs were prepared via the one-step PLAL method using graphite in high-purity ethanol with DETA. In a typical process, 500 mg of natural graphite flakes were dispersed in 200 ml of ethanol and DETA 5 mol% solutions. High-power sonication was subsequently performed during laser ablation for vigorous dispersion of the graphite. Laser ablation was performed on the graphite suspension for 30 min at room temperature and in the air using a Q-Switch ND:YAG laser system. The graphite suspension was ablated by a horizontal pulsed laser beam (355 nm, third harmonic) at a repetition rate of 10 Hz. The pulse width was 10 nm, and the ablation energy was 1 W. After completion of the PLAL treatment, the N-doped GQDs suspension was dried overnight at 80 °C.

HR-TEM images of the pristine GQDs and N-doped GQDs samples were captured with a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope equipped with a field emission gun (200 kV; Jeol, USA). XPS spectra were recorded for both samples using a VG ESCALAB 220i system (Thermo Scientific, USA). XPS survey and high-resolution scans were performed at pass energies of 100 and 20 eV, respectively. The X-ray beam size was approximately 100 µm. pristine GQDs and N-doped GQDs samples for XPS measurement were prepared on silicon (Si) substrate via a spin coating technique with the rotation speed adjusted to 3000 rpm. The samples were dried at room temperature for 2 h prior to measurement. Room temperature PL spectra were collected using a photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Fluoromax-plus with 150 W xenon arc lamp) in the wavelength range from 300 nm to 800 nm. The excitation wavelength used for measuring the PL emission spectra was 400, 450 and 500 nm. A time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon) was used to measure nanosecond lifetime.

Quinine sulfate in water (QY = 0.58) and fluorescein in ethanol (QY = 0.79) were chosen as the standards for measuring the QY of excited pristine GQDs and N-doped GQDs. The QY of pristine GQDs and N-doped GQDs was calculated as follows according to:
Φ x
=
Φ
s t
( I
x
/
I
s t
)
( η
x
2
/
η
s t
2
)
( A
s t
/
A x
)
where Ф is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript ‘st’ refers to the standard with the known QY and ‘x’ for the sample. To minimize the reabsorption effects, absorption in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette was kept below 0.10 at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The above experimental sections follow our previous work [14].

Fig. 1 shows the optical properties of the pristine GQDs (PGQDs), N-doped GQDs with high-power sonication (on-NGQDs), and N-doped GQDs without high-power sonication (off-NGQDs). The UV–Vis spectra show typically characterized absorption bands at 210, 260, and ∼350 nm as shown in Fig. 1(a). Generally, the absorption bands at 210 and 260 nm are attributed to the π → π* transition of CC of the sp2 C domain in the sp3 C matrix, and the shoulder peak at 320–350 nm in the absorption spectra is assigned to the n → π* transition of CO. Compared to the PGQDs, the N-doped GQDs show much stronger UV–Vis spectra at the π → π* transition (210 and 260 nm), indicating the high density of the zig-zag sites formed in the GQDs after the N doping [25–27]. Also, the absorption band at 320–350 nm in the oxygen functional groups is slightly increased after the N doping. Qu and co-workers reported that their oxygen reduction experiment for N-doped GQDs resulted in almost no change in the UV–Vis spectra at 320–350 nm. Thus, this shoulder peak is not only related to the n → π* transition of CO but is also related to the transition of CN or CN [27].

To further demonstrate the effect of N doping, we carried out PL and PL excitation (PLE) analysis as shown in Fig. 1b and c. Compared to the PGQDs, the N-doped GQDs exhibited strong PL emission and a slight blue shift after the N doping as well as QY values calculated at 0.83%, 4.2% and 9.1% for the PGQDs, on-NGQDs, and off-NGQDs, respectively. Contrary to our expectations, the PL properties of the off-NGQDs (without sonication) were superior to those of the on-NGQDs (with sonication), as is discussed in the XPS analysis section. The relatively strong electron affinity of the N atoms (strong withdrawing groups) in the GQDs could contribute to the PL enhancement and blue shift [22,23]. Previously reported observations and quantum mechanical calculations proved the strong electron-withdrawing ability of the N atoms within the conjugated C plane [18]. Thus, the successful doping of substitutional N results in more efficient PL radiative emission by the high-density electrons in the sp2 domains. Also, the three types of GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL emission as seen in Fig. S1, which shows similar PL properties to those previously reported [10,21,27]. In addition, the PLE spectra of the N-doped GQDs are clearly different from those of the PGQDs. The PLE spectra of the N-doped GQDs show two distinct peaks at 260 and ∼355 nm, which are correlated with the two transitions at 255 nm (4.76 eV) and 355 nm (3.49 eV). Hoffmann and co-workers demonstrated that the carbene ground-state multiplicity is related to the energy differences (δE) between the σ and π orbitals. The δE should be below 1.5 eV for a triplet ground state. In our work, the δE was calculated as 1.27 eV for the N-doped GQDs, which is below the critical value of 1.5 eV. Thus, the strong blue emission of the N-doped GQDs can be attributed to the intrinsic state in the triplet carbene groups [30,31]. Compared to the N-doped GQDs, the PGQDs showed weak and broad PLE spectra, indicating that the emission of the PGQDs originated from the dominant surface states (i.e., derived from the oxygen-rich functional groups) [32].

We investigated the chemical composition of the three types of GQDs by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 (a) shows the XPS full survey spectra of the PGQDs, on-NGQDs, and off-NGQDs. The XPS spectra of the three types of GQDs show two distinct peaks at ∼284 and ∼529 eV, which represent the C1s and O1s, respectively [27–29]. Interestingly enough, the N1s peak located at ∼399 eV was present in the XPS spectra of the on-NGQDs and off-NGQDs, indicating the effective incorporation of N atoms in the GQDs via the PLAL process. In addition, the off-NGQDs represents an estimated N/C atomic ratio as high as 15%, two times higher than that of the on-NGQDs (7%). When N atoms are incorporated into carbon materials, there are typically three types of configurations for the CN bonding (pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic configurations) as shown in Fig. 2b. Pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N refer to the sp2 hybridized bonding with two C atoms, the sp3 hybridized in the five-atom ring, and the replacement of C in the graphene lattice with N, respectively. To determine the CN configuration in the on-NGQDs (Fig. 2c) and off-NGQDS (Fig. 2d), the N1s peak of the XPS spectra was analyzed. The N1s peak of the N-GQDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 397.8, 399.5, and 400.5 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N, respectively [26,28]. It was found that the off-NGQDs exhibit a higher concentration for pyridinic N and graphitic N bonding, which can enhance the PL intensity in the GQDs [23]. These high-resolution XPS results reveal that the doping of GQDs by substitution with N can be suppressed by a sonication-assisted pulsed laser ablation process. Thus, this phenomenon is related to the optical properties due to the difference in the N concentration (e.g., pyridinic N, graphitic N) by ultrasonication. Also, Fig. S2 shows the C1s peak in the XPS spectra of the PGQDs, on-NGQDs, and off-NGQDs. The carbon peak can be deconvoluted into four peaks centered at 284.5, 285.8, 286.4, and 278.8 eV, which represent CC/CC, hydroxyl groups, CN/CN, and carboxyl groups, respectively [20,22]. The above XPS results indicate that the classic PLAL process (without sonication) is more effective for N atoms incorporated into GQDs.

The proposed mechanism for the transformation of graphite flakes to off-NGQDs via the PLAL process is shown in Fig. 3 a. The nanosecond pulsed laser phenomenon is typically associated with the generation of a plasma plume, cavitation bubbles, and thermal decomposition near the surface of the target. These phenomena are correlated to the thermal heating, melting, and evaporation by laser ablation [15,33–35] (Fig. 3a). Ex-situ HR-TEM analysis was undertaken to investigate the change in the graphite flake morphology from graphite to off-NGQDs, as shown in Fig. 3b–d. The pulse laser irradiation injected on the surface of the graphite flakes, which can lead to the generation of carbon clusters (i.e., derived from the graphite flakes) and N molecules (i.e., derived from the DETA) in cavitation bubbles. These carbon clusters and N molecules are unstable because of their high surface energy and they tend to aggregate. Also, the carbon and N aggregates prefer to form a two-dimensional structure (N-doped graphene nanosheet) due to the lower formation energy of the hexagonal structure of carbon (Fig. 3c) [14]. The graphene nanosheet derived from the graphite flakes shows the highly crystalline structure of the graphene sheet with a distinct lattice fringe (0.24 nm) as shown in Fig. S3. The graphene nanosheets are further ablated by the injected pulsed laser to form several nanosized off-NGQDs as shown in Fig. 3c and d. In the case of the on-NGQDs, the exposure time of the graphite flakes to the laser ablation can be reduced by the anomalous convection of the graphite flakes via the high-power sonication. These anomalous conditions are effectively suppressed by thermal heating of the absorbed pulsed laser energy into the graphite flakes during the laser ablation. This phenomenon could hinder the thermal decomposition of various precursors (e.g., carbon and N) and the N incorporation into the carbon matrix [15]. As a result, the classic PLAL process (without sonication) can effectively facilitate the decomposition of DETA, and the easy formation of N-doped GQDs (Fig. 2 and Fig. S2).

Fig. 4 shows HR-TEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) images of the GQDs prepared by using pulsed laser ablation for 30 min. The PGQDs (Fig. 4a and c) and off-NGQDs (Fig. 4b and d) have average diameters of less than 6 nm with homogeneous sizes and shapes. The average diameters of PGQDs and off-NGQDs were obtained by counting about 40 particles. The size distribution of both the PGQDs and off-NGQDs was fitted by Gaussian curve with 95% confidence interval (Fig. S4). In addition, the average particle size of the on-NGQDs was similar to that of the above PGQDs and off-NGQDs (not shown this article). According to the fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns (right insets, Fig. 4c and d), it is observed that both types of GQDs are crystallized in the pristine graphene structure. The interlayer spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the lattice fringe of graphene, as shown in the left insets of Fig. 4c and d, and is similar to that previously reported [14]. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) data are shown in Fig. S5, which illustrates the topographic morphology and the height distribution of the PGQDs, on-NGQDs, and off-NGQDs. Statistical analysis reveals that more than 87% of the PGQDs, on-NGQDs and off-NGQDs have a thickness between 0.5 and 1.8 nm, corresponding to 3–4 graphene layers (Fig. S5a–c). This results indicating that the produced three types of GQDs have a monolayer structure or one with several layers, which agrees with the previous reports [12,13].

In general, the origin of the optical properties of the GQDs remains an important question, which is tentatively suggested to be related to emissive traps, aromatic structures, and the density of the free zig-zag sites [36,37]. To investigate the recombination mechanism of the three types of GQDs, we performed time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy (Fig. 5 a–c). The fluorescence decay curve is fitted with a tri-exponential function (Eq. (1)), where the fluorescence decay occurs through three different relaxation pathways as follows:
(1)
fit = A +
B 1
e
( - t / τ 1 )
+
B 2
e
( - t / τ 2 )
+
B 3
e
( - t / τ 3 )
,
where ‘τ’ is the fluorescence lifetime and ‘B’ represents the amplitude of the corresponding lifetime. The obtained chi-square (χ2) value ranges between 1.2 and 1.3. The χ2 value in the range of 1.0 < X2 < 1.4 is generally assumed to be acceptable for fitting. The decays were monitored at their optimal emission wavelengths, and the best-fit parameters are listed in Table 1 . The lifetimes of the PGQDs are τ1 = 1.5 ns (17%), τ2 = 4.6 ns (59%), and τ3 = 15 ns (24%); those of the on-NGQDs are τ1 = 0.9 ns (27%), τ2 = 3.3 ns (56%), and τ3 = 9.1 ns (17%), and those of the off-NGQDs are τ1 = 0.7 ns (33%), τ2 = 2.5 ns (56%), and τ3 = 9.8 ns (11%). In general, the emission originating from a surface state shows a longer recombination lifetime than that from an intrinsic state [32]. As a result, the fraction of the short lifetimes (τ1) from the intrinsic states in the triplet carbene groups was dramatically increased after N doping. On the other hand, the fraction of the long lifetimes (τ2 and 3) from the surface states was gradually decreased with the N incorporation. Based on the above results, the N-doped GQDs give rise to more efficient PL radiative emission due to increasing the quantitative fraction of the short lifetime. Consequently, the N-doped GQDs show a remarkable enhancement of the optical properties as shown in Fig. 1.

We successfully demonstrated that N-doped GQDs with excellent optical properties (e.g. QY, PL intensity) can be achieved via the PLAL process using abundantly available and inexpensive graphite flakes as the carbon precursor. The nitrogen atoms can be more easily doped into the GQDs when the classic PLAL process (without sonication) is employed, resulting in the greatly enhanced QY of the off-NGQDs (0.8% → 9.1%) after doping with N. The three different types of N bonding (pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N) were formed in the N-doped GQDs. It was found that the enhancement of optical properties by the N doping is mainly attributed to the pyridinic N and graphitic N configurations. We also proposed a possible formation mechanism of the N-doped GQDs based on the plasma plume, cavitation bubble collapse, and thermal decomposition model. The recombination mechanism of the N-doped GQDs was investigated by time-resolved photoluminescence. The N doping leading to the enhanced PL intensity was explained by the increased fraction of the short lifetime from the intrinsic states. Also, the fraction of the long lifetime from the surface states (e.g., oxygen functional groups) leads to the poor optical properties. We consider that our strategy is a simple and low-cost way to achieve high quantum yield N-doped GQDs via the PLAL process, allowing for promising applications particularly in the optoelectronic and bioimaging fields.

Sukhyun Kang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Young Kyu Jeong: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Jeong Ho Ryu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yong Son: Investigation, Data curation. Won Rae Kim: Formal analysis, Data curation. Byoungsoo Lee: Data curation, Formal analysis. Kyung Hwan Jung: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Kang Min Kim: Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Korea Institute of Industrial Technology as “The Project on Developing Technology of the multi-functional additive manufacturing and Supporting the SMEs of Applications” (KITECH JC-19-0003).
